segments.title: Live question system setup
segments.summary: The stream explains how audience questions should be submitted so they can be tracked rather than lost in chat.
segments.body: This chapter is useful archive structure evidence: live-stream pages need Q&A segments that can preserve the prompt, timestamp, and source context separately from ordinary topical notes.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses adding questions to the question system.

segments.title: Modern Navy equivalent of the Blackburn Blackburn
segments.question: What is the modern Navy equivalent of the Blackburn Blackburn for this channel?
segments.answerShort: The prototype keeps this as a timestamped Q&A seed for later transcript-backed answer cleanup.
segments.summary: A live Q&A prompt asks for a modern Navy analogue to the Blackburn Blackburn, giving the prototype a real `qa` segment to render and search.
segments.body: This segment deliberately records the question and source window without over-polishing the answer. It proves that Q&A can remain a segment subtype while still carrying question-specific fields.

evidence.note: The transcript contains the modern Navy equivalent question and setup.

segments.title: Spanish 1950s destroyer escorts
segments.summary: A discussion of Spanish Navy destroyer escorts frames them as useful ships, but as gun-and-torpedo designs in a naval world moving beyond that model.
segments.body: The segment shows how ordinary subject notes should work: it is not a fabricated Q&A entry, but a topical point with timestamped transcript evidence and tags for search and browsing.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Spanish Navy destroyer escorts and gun-and-torpedo destroyer design.

segments.title: Carrier group force structure sketch
segments.summary: A force-structure answer sketches a carrier group built around carriers, LHDs, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and submarines.
segments.body: This segment tests multi-topic browsing and search metadata: the same timestamp belongs under carrier groups, naval aviation, destroyers, and modern navy planning.

evidence.note: The transcript lists carriers, LHDs, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and SSNs around a carrier group.

segments.title: Ideal destroyers depend on era, but screening stays constant
segments.summary: The opening frames the ideal destroyer as era-dependent while keeping screening and task-group protection as the through-line.
segments.body: This is a useful organizing segment for destroyer coverage: the transcript separates offensive firepower from the more persistent role of protecting fleets, task groups, and ships from large and small threats.

evidence.note: Opening thesis explains that ideal destroyers change by decade but remain centered on screening and protection.

segments.title: Durand de la Penne as a destroyer and Oto Melara 76 mm story
segments.summary: The class is introduced as both an Italian Cold War destroyer subject and a way to follow the Oto Melara 76 mm from ship-design perspective.
segments.body: The source window links the class to Italian destroyer development and then moves into its missile and gun fit. It is a good first-pass anchor for later deeper coverage of the 76 mm, Otomat, and ship layout choices.

evidence.note: Transcript introduces the class as an Oto Melara 76 mm story and outlines the ship weapons fit.

segments.title: Near-term spending matters more than distant Defence Investment Plan claims
segments.summary: The live review argues that decade-ahead plan claims should be weighed against what is funded in the next six months to a year.
segments.body: The segment is an organizing hook for current-affairs coverage: long-range statements about future ships can be overtaken by elections and governments, while near-term spending decisions are more concrete.

evidence.note: Transcript contrasts ten-year plan claims with near-term spending and mentions Type 83 timing in relation to Type 26.

segments.title: Pre-war destroyer procurement as a preparedness problem
segments.summary: The video opens with Mahan and preparedness, using the delay in creating war material as the frame for pre-WWI destroyer procurement.
segments.body: This segment grounds the episode in the policy problem behind too-few destroyers: ships cannot be postponed until hostilities are imminent, especially under modern construction timelines.

evidence.note: Opening cites Mahan and preparedness before connecting the argument to destroyer debate and naval construction timing.

segments.title: Bruships 248 question intake reopens during the live stream
segments.summary: The transcript shows the host reopening questions and waiting for the on-screen banner before continuing intake.
segments.body: This first-pass segment records live-stream structure only. Bruships 248 still needs a dedicated Q&A extraction pass to turn audience prompts into useful topical segments.

evidence.note: Transcript says questions are open and references waiting for the banner before proceeding.

segments.title: Yak-38 as the overlooked Cold War V/STOL fighter
segments.summary: The opening positions the Yak-38 as the other Cold War V/STOL fighter, often overshadowed by the Harrier but not simply a failure.
segments.body: The first-pass value is the comparison frame: the transcript notes that the F-35B hover arrangement is closer to Yak-38 engineering than to Harrier, while still calling the Forger a first-generation carrier fighter.

evidence.note: Opening compares Yak-38 with Harrier and F-35B while setting up its first-generation V/STOL role.

segments.title: Press gang myth and reality are separated from the start
segments.summary: The episode opens by contrasting the popular myth of random press-gang seizures with the operational reality of fleet manpower needs.
segments.body: The window gives the page a manpower-policy anchor: press activity is tied to the active fighting season and then narrowed from popular mythology toward how impressment actually operated.

evidence.note: Transcript introduces the topic, active-season fleet growth, the myth, and the first statement of reality.

segments.title: Japanese destroyer fleet is framed around always building
segments.summary: The opening rejects a mysterious Japanese procurement method and instead points to continuous shipbuilding as the practical explanation.
segments.body: This segment improves browsing for modern fleet structure and procurement: the Japan case is presented as a replacement-cycle discipline, not a hidden political trick.

evidence.note: Opening discusses Japanese procurement claims and identifies the method as always building.

segments.title: Bruships 247 opens questions across Discord, Twitch, and YouTube
segments.summary: The transcript marks questions as open across the live platforms, establishing the stream as a Q&A source that needs later extraction.
segments.body: This is a structural segment rather than a substantive naval answer. It keeps the live-stream mechanics visible while preserving the need for a later Q&A pass.

evidence.note: Transcript says questions are open on Discord, Twitch, and YouTube and mentions the bot.

segments.title: HMS Tiger 15-inch upgrade counterfactual starts from the real ship constraints
segments.summary: The episode explicitly splits historical setup from alternate history before moving into HMS Tiger gun and barbette constraints.
segments.body: This first-pass segment marks the format and the technical basis for the counterfactual: the later scenario is meant to rest on real history and ship-design constraints.

evidence.note: Opening explains the history-first structure and introduces the 13.5-inch barbette issue.

segments.title: British Cold War destroyers begin with many unbuilt design studies
segments.summary: The opening frames British Cold War destroyers through the Royal Navy habit of producing many paper and concept studies.
segments.body: The segment anchors later deeper curation around force planning and unbuilt designs: the transcript presents the subject as logical design work for a war that did not happen.

evidence.note: Transcript introduces British Cold War destroyers and emphasizes paper studies and concept designs.

segments.title: Defence Investment Plan live review is conditional on publication
segments.summary: The live stream sets expectations: review the plan if it has appeared, or discuss what should be in it if it has not.
segments.body: This source window is a good first-pass current-affairs anchor because it records the format and contingency before the long live review moves into details.

evidence.note: Opening welcomes viewers to a live review if published or a discussion of what should be included if not.

segments.title: Bruships 246 waits for the questions-open sign
segments.summary: The stream setup tells viewers not to post questions until the questions-open sign is visible.
segments.body: The first-pass segment captures the live intake boundary. The actual naval Q&A content should be extracted in a later pass from the long stream.

evidence.note: Transcript advises waiting for the questions-open sign and then continues stream setup.

segments.title: Fleet organization terminology overview
segments.summary: The episode is introduced as a concise explanation of fleet organization titles and what different groupings mean.
segments.body: The source window makes this a durable reference page for fleet, squadron, flotilla, and related organizational terms rather than a one-off video note.

evidence.note: Opening states the video explains fleet organization and begins defining fleet as a whole navy or constituent part.

segments.title: Oliver Hazard Perry life-extension question sets the live topic
segments.summary: The host frames the central question as whether the US Navy could have modernized Perry-class frigates until a replacement arrived.
segments.body: This is the first usable content window after stream setup and gives the page a clear procurement and modernization anchor for later chaptering.

evidence.note: Transcript states the question about retrofitting and modernizing Oliver Hazard Perry frigates for extended service.

segments.title: Bruships 245 waits for the Discord questions-open signal
segments.summary: The transcript shows the host waiting for the Discord questions-open signal before changing the live banner.
segments.body: This segment is deliberately structural. It marks where the live Q&A system becomes available, while leaving the actual alternate-history and naval-history answers for a later extraction pass.

evidence.note: Transcript references the Discord questions-open signal and banner change.

segments.title: HMS Hermes cruiser-carrier scenario starts from a patron prompt
segments.summary: The video frames a counterfactual around Britain building ten small Hermes-style carriers between 1926 and 1930.
segments.body: The first-pass segment captures why this belongs in both alternate-history and naval-aviation organization: the prompt is treated as a scenario that needs a historically viable path.

evidence.note: Opening reads the small aircraft carrier prompt and states that a viable historical scenario exists.

segments.title: Ram Dass Katari is introduced through modern Indian Navy development
segments.summary: The opening links the Indian destroyer program and indigenization to Katari as a founder figure for the modern Indian Navy.
segments.body: This segment gives the site a leadership path into Indian naval modernization, tying the biography to shipbuilding capacity and destroyer development rather than leaving it as only a person page.

evidence.note: Opening connects Indian destroyers, indigenization, shipbuilding capacity, and Katari as founder of the modern Indian Navy.

segments.title: Combining MEKO and StanFlex raises the problem of too much modularity
segments.summary: The opening asks whether combining a modular frigate design with a modular containerized weapon system would be too much modularity.
segments.body: This first-pass segment creates a stable home for MEKO, StanFlex, and modular ship-design searches. Later chaptering can separate the historical programs from the combined counterfactual.

evidence.note: Opening describes combining modular frigate design with StanFlex and names the resulting thought experiment.

segments.title: Constellation class shifted from conservative design to poor value proposition
segments.summary: The episode asks why a seemingly prudent off-the-shelf frigate became described as 60 percent of a Burke for 80 percent of the cost.
segments.body: This segment gives the page a procurement anchor: the transcript immediately turns from program outcome to US naval procurement incentives, perfection-seeking, risk aversion, and requirements.

evidence.note: Opening states the 60 percent capability for 80 percent cost framing and links it to US naval procurement incentives.
